lesson.name: Plato: Dialogues & the Republic - Lesson 2 — Crito

tasks.chapterTitle: Introduction to Plato and The Apology
The Crito: Duty to the State
The Phaedo: Arguments for the Immortality of the Soul
Theory of Forms and Final Arguments
The Death of Socrates and Closing

messages.content:
Lesson 2 — Crito - Presentation24 Slides
The Primary Design of the Crito
  • Plato's primary design in this dialogue is to free Socrates from the imputation of having attempted to corrupt the Athenian youth.
  • The dialogue also works to establish the principle that under all circumstances it is the duty of a good citizen to obey the laws of his country.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the introduction, what is the primary design of the Crito?
options: To establish unconditional civic obedience to the laws
To free Socrates from the imputation of corrupting the youth
To argue for escape from prison
To discuss the immortality of the soul
questions.explanation: The source states the primary design is to free Socrates from the imputation of having attempted to corrupt the Athenian youth.

questions.question: What principle does the Crito establish about a good citizen?
options: A citizen may disobey unjust laws
Under all circumstances it is the duty of a good citizen to obey the laws of his country
Citizens should return evil for evil when harmed
Civic duty is weaker than family ties
questions.explanation: The source directly states the principle that under all circumstances it is the duty of a good citizen to obey the laws of his country.

questions.question: To what relations are the obligations of a citizen compared?
options: Friend to friend and neighbor to neighbor
Child to its parent or a slave to his master
Judge to defendant and accuser to accused
Philosopher to student
questions.explanation: The source says the obligations of a citizen to his country are even more binding than those of a child to its parent, or a slave to his master.

questions.question: What divine principle does Socrates establish before discussing civic duty?
options: It is right to return evil for evil
It is wrong to return evil for evil
Escape is always permitted
Laws may be ignored in private
questions.explanation: The source records that Socrates establishes the divine principle that it is wrong to return evil for evil.

questions.question: How does the dialogue conclude regarding Socrates' decision?
options: Socrates resolves to escape
Crito persuades Socrates to flee
Socrates resolves to submit himself to the will of Providence
The laws are declared unjust
questions.explanation: The source states that at length Crito admits he has no answer and Socrates resolves to submit himself to the will of Providence.

cards.front: What is the primary design of the Crito?
cards.back: To free Socrates from the imputation of having attempted to corrupt the Athenian youth.

cards.front: What principle does the Crito establish about obeying laws?
cards.back: Under all circumstances it is the duty of a good citizen to obey the laws of his country.

cards.front: How do the two designs of the dialogue relate?
cards.back: They are closely interwoven so the general principle is illustrated by Socrates' example.

cards.front: What divine principle guides the discussion of civic duty?
cards.back: It is wrong to return evil for evil.

cards.front: To what is the citizen's obligation to country compared?
cards.back: Even more binding than those of a child to its parent or a slave to his master.

cards.front: What must a citizen do according to the established principle?
cards.back: Obey the established laws at whatever cost to himself.

cards.front: What does Crito admit at the end of the discussion?
cards.back: That he has no answer to make.

cards.front: What does Socrates resolve after the discussion?
cards.back: To submit himself to the will of Providence.

cards.front: Why does Socrates refuse prepared means of escape?
cards.back: Because escape would violate the duty to obey the laws.

cards.front: What does Socrates' conduct demonstrate?
cards.back: The binding force of the laws even when they lead to death.

slides.title: The Primary Design of the Crito
bullets: Plato's primary design in this dialogue is to free Socrates from the imputation of having attempted to corrupt the Athenian youth.
The dialogue also works to establish the principle that under all circumstances it is the duty of a good citizen to obey the laws of his country.
In this way the dialogue shows that Socrates never urged the young to defy authority but instead modeled how a citizen remains loyal to the city even when its verdict falls upon him personally.
By accepting execution rather than fleeing, Socrates makes plain that the obligation to law cannot be set aside without destroying the very bond that holds any lawful community together.
slides.notes: Drawn directly from the introduction to the Crito on p.14.

slides.title: The Secondary Design of the Crito
bullets: The secondary design is to establish that under all circumstances it is the duty of a good citizen to obey the laws of his country.
These two designs are closely interwoven so that the general principle appears illustrated by the example of Socrates.

When Socrates refuses escape and accepts the hemlock, the dialogue reveals that civic duty overrides even the claim of personal innocence, turning obedience into a lived demonstration that protects the city from dissolving into private wills.

This absolute allegiance therefore carries the consequence that a citizen may suffer unjustly yet still strengthen the rule of law, showing how individual fate becomes subordinate to the enduring authority that makes collective justice possible.
slides.notes: Direct from the Stallbaum remark in the source on p.14.

slides.title: Crito as Friend and Visitor
bullets: Crito was one of those friends of Socrates who had been present at his trial and had offered to assist in paying a fine.
Crito appears to have frequently visited his friend in prison after his condemnation.
These repeated prison visits show how friendship can quietly urge a man to place loyalty above the city's sentence, turning private affection into a living pressure against public law.

By remaining close at hand Crito embodies the human cost of civic obedience, forcing the question whether justice requires a friend to accept his companion's death or to help him flee and thereby break the bond that holds the city together.
slides.notes: Background on Crito's role from p.14.

slides.title: Crito's Early Morning Visit
bullets: Crito obtained access to Socrates' cell very early in the morning and found him composed in a quiet sleep.
Crito brings intelligence that the ship whose arrival would be the signal for Socrates' death is expected to arrive forthwith.

The early hour of Crito’s arrival underscores how Athenian procedures convert even private friendship into an instrument of public law, compelling Crito to deliver the city’s timetable before Socrates has awakened.

By announcing that the fatal ship is already near, Crito places Socrates at the precise intersection where personal affection must either yield to civic obligation or openly defy the legal sentence that the city has pronounced.
slides.notes: Sets the scene from the source on p.14.

slides.title: Crito Entreats Escape
bullets: Crito takes occasion to entreat Socrates to make his escape, the means of which were already prepared.
Socrates promises to follow the advice of Crito if after the matter had been fully discussed it should appear to be right to do so.
Crito's ready preparations expose the pull of private loyalty against the city's claim on every citizen, so that affection itself becomes a test of whether justice can ever yield to personal ties.
Socrates' refusal to act without full argument shows that civic duty must be examined in the light of reason, lest an unexamined escape injure the very principle that obliges obedience to law.
slides.notes: From the introduction on p.14.

slides.title: Proposal to Consider Civic Duty
bullets: Socrates proposes to consider the duty of a citizen toward his country.
He establishes the divine principle that it is wrong to return evil for evil.
This principle binds the citizen to endure even an unjust penalty rather than strike back, lest the city itself dissolve into cycles of private vengeance that no law can contain.
The duty therefore demands that one either persuade the city of its error through lawful means or accept its judgment, placing personal integrity in direct service to the common order.
slides.notes: Core transition to the argument on p.14.

slides.title: Wrong to Return Evil for Evil
bullets: Socrates goes on to show the obligations of a citizen after establishing that it is wrong to return evil for evil.
"It is wrong to return evil for evil" is presented as a divine principle that must guide civic conduct.

This rule therefore places the citizen under a lifelong duty to endure even wrongful injury from the city rather than strike back, lest the shared order that reared him be torn apart by private vengeance.

The result is a one-sided bond in which the individual may try to persuade the laws of their error yet must never repay harm with harm, turning justice into an obligation that protects the whole at the cost of personal satisfaction.
slides.notes: Verbatim principle highlighted from p.14.

slides.title: Obligations More Binding Than Parental
bullets: The obligations of a citizen to his country are even more binding than those of a child to its parent.
This comparison makes concrete the strength of the citizen's duty to obey the established laws.
slides.notes: Direct comparison from the source on p.14.

slides.title: Obligations More Binding Than Master-Slave
bullets: The obligations of a citizen to his country are even more binding than those of a slave to his master.
Therefore it is the citizen's duty to obey the established laws at whatever cost to himself.
This deeper tie means the citizen cannot treat the laws as optional tools to be discarded when they become inconvenient or painful.
The result is a stark choice in which personal ruin must be endured so that the city itself does not dissolve into private bargains and private escapes.
slides.notes: Direct comparison from the source on p.14.

slides.title: Duty to Obey at Whatever Cost
bullets: It is the duty of a good citizen to obey the laws of his country under all circumstances.
Socrates must therefore obey the established laws at whatever cost to himself.
Yielding to an unjust sentence therefore preserves the city’s authority for every future citizen, even while it exacts the ultimate personal price from the one who obeys.
To place private survival above the law would fracture the mutual bond that alone makes ordered justice possible among free people.
slides.notes: Core thesis repeated in concrete terms from p.14.

slides.title: Crito Admits No Answer
bullets: At length Crito admits that he has no answer to make to the arguments presented.
Socrates resolves to submit himself to the will of Providence.
Crito’s silence therefore confirms that no private plea can lawfully override the city’s reasoned claim upon its citizens.
Socrates’ acceptance of providence shows that genuine civic duty may require surrendering one’s life rather than fracturing the lawful order that has sustained it.
slides.notes: Conclusion of the discussion on p.14.

slides.title: Socrates Resolves to Submit
bullets: Socrates resolves to submit himself to the will of Providence after the discussion.
This submission follows from the established duty of the citizen to obey the laws.

In yielding thus, the philosopher shows that the soul's allegiance to truth must still honor the city's claim upon the body, lest private judgment dissolve the common bond that makes shared inquiry possible at all.

This choice therefore carries a quiet cost: the just man may suffer under laws he has not broken, yet by enduring them he keeps intact the authority that alone can shelter future generations of citizens seeking the good.
slides.notes: Final resolution from p.14.

slides.title: Laws Illustrated by Socrates' Example
bullets: The general principle of civic obedience appears only to be illustrated by the example of Socrates.
Socrates' own conduct demonstrates the binding force of the laws even when they lead to death.
By submitting to an unjust sentence Socrates turns his death into a living proof that no citizen may exempt himself from the law without undermining the city that shelters him.
This stance creates a lasting tension between the claims of individual conscience and the need for uniform obedience that alone keeps collective justice intact.
slides.notes: From the interwoven designs on p.14.

slides.title: Civic Obligation Stronger Than Personal Ties
bullets: The citizen's obligation to the country surpasses even the strong ties of child to parent.
This hierarchy shows why escape would violate justice and law.

When the citizen places the city first, every private affection including the bond to parents gains its security from that larger order, so to flee is to treat the source of all protection as optional and thereby weaken the very shelter others still depend upon.

This ranking of duties forces a lasting tension inside the soul, where personal love must continually yield to the impartial demands of law or else the individual becomes the exception that invites everyone to place feeling above justice.
slides.notes: Elaborates the comparison on p.14.

slides.title: Civic Obligation Stronger Than Servitude
bullets: The citizen's obligation to the country surpasses even the relation of slave to master.
Obedience therefore remains required regardless of personal cost or danger.

This priority of duty means the citizen cannot treat the city as one master among many, for every benefit of life, from nurture to protection, flows from its laws and cannot be repaid by escape or defiance.
The result is a stark tension: personal safety and even life itself must yield whenever the laws demand it, lest the entire order that makes citizenship possible collapse into private bargaining.
slides.notes: Elaborates the comparison on p.14.

slides.title: Full Discussion Required Before Action
bullets: Socrates will follow Crito's advice only if after full discussion it appears right to do so.
This insistence on reasoned examination upholds justice before any decision about the laws.
Acting without that shared scrutiny invites the very injustice one hopes to escape, because private bonds can quietly override the common good the laws are meant to protect.

The demand for full examination therefore converts civic obedience from mere habit into an active partnership between reason and the city, where every citizen bears responsibility for testing what loyalty truly requires.
slides.notes: From Socrates' promise on p.14.

slides.title: Laws Must Be Obeyed Even Unto Death
bullets: The established laws must be obeyed at whatever cost to oneself.
Socrates therefore accepts the sentence rather than attempting escape.

When a citizen chooses death over flight, the laws retain their binding force across generations and the city avoids sliding into a place where every person decides for himself which verdicts to honor.
Such obedience places the good of the shared order above personal safety, showing that justice is not merely a bargain one keeps only while it is convenient.
slides.notes: Direct consequence from p.14.

slides.title: Justice Requires Submission to Law
bullets: Justice demands that the citizen not return evil for evil even when the laws appear harsh.
Socrates models this by refusing to violate the laws of Athens.
By accepting an adverse verdict rather than fleeing or retaliating, the citizen keeps the city’s shared rules intact and thereby protects the very framework in which future just dealings can occur.

Refusal to exempt oneself from the law therefore carries the concrete consequence that private judgment yields to public order, lest every individual become judge in his own case and the common life collapse.
slides.notes: Connects the divine principle to action on p.14.

slides.title: Crito's Means Prepared in Vain
bullets: Although the means of escape were already prepared, Socrates declines after discussion.
The decision rests solely on whether escape would violate civic obligation.
By refusing the escape that Crito has arranged, Socrates demonstrates that friendship and personal safety must yield whenever they conflict with the deeper claim the city holds over its citizens.

The result is that any practical plan, however carefully laid, loses all force once the question of justice is posed, leaving the citizen bound to endure the law's verdict rather than fracture the order that sustains collective life.
slides.notes: From the scene on p.14.

slides.title: Providence and Civic Duty United
bullets: Socrates resolves to submit himself to the will of Providence.
This submission coincides with unwavering obedience to the laws of the city.

In this alignment the individual discovers that yielding to the city's verdict safeguards an inner order no private flight could preserve, turning apparent powerlessness into a public witness that the laws themselves remain worthy of reverence.

Such fidelity carries a lasting civic consequence: when citizens treat legal obligation as an expression of providence, the city gains stability even in its errors, while the soul avoids the deeper injustice of placing self-will above the common bond.
slides.notes: Final unity of themes on p.14.

slides.title: The Dialogue Establishes Unconditional Obedience
bullets: The Crito establishes the principle that under all circumstances it is the duty of a good citizen to obey the laws.
No exception is granted even when obedience leads to death.
This absolute duty binds each citizen to the city as if to a parent whose authority cannot be renounced without destroying the very bond that makes ordered life possible.

The result is that personal survival must yield whenever it conflicts with the law, lest the example of selective obedience erode the shared foundation on which every future verdict, just or unjust, rests.
slides.notes: Summary thesis from p.14.

slides.title: Socrates' Conduct Embodies the Principle
bullets: Socrates' conduct in prison embodies the principle that a citizen must obey the laws at whatever cost.
His refusal to escape illustrates justice, law, and civic obligation in action.

By remaining to face execution rather than fleeing, Socrates demonstrates that obedience preserves the authority of law for every future citizen, turning one man's fate into a safeguard for the city's enduring order.

This stance reveals the sharp tension that civic obligation can override even the pull of self-preservation, showing how justice gains its force only when individuals accept its demands without exception.
slides.notes: Concrete embodiment from p.14.
